title: ENH: Add `df.corrupt()` accessor to inject random faults and typos for Data Science education
ENH: Improve missing column KeyError message in DataFrame access
body:
### Feature Type

- [ ] Adding new functionality to pandas

- [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas

- [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas


### Problem Description

# Problem Description
Currently, when users attempt to access a column that does not exist in a DataFrame using:

df["Age"]

Pandas raises a generic error message:

KeyError: 'Age'

While technically correct, this message provides very limited context for users, especially beginners.

The current behavior does not:

explain why the error occurred,
show available columns,
help users identify possible typos,
improve debugging experience.

This can make debugging slower and more frustrating for new users working with DataFrames.

Example Reproduction
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Name": ["Pradip", "Rahul"]
})

print(df["Age"])

Current output:

KeyError: 'Age'

# Expected Behavior

The error message should provide more contextual information.

Possible improved output:

KeyError: Column 'Age' not found in DataFrame.
Available columns: ['Name']

OR:

KeyError: Column 'Age' does not exist.
Did you mean: 'Name'?

### Feature Description

# Feature Description

Enhance the DataFrame column-access error handling mechanism to produce more descriptive and user-friendly error messages when a requested column is missing.

Potential improvements include:

  1.displaying available column names,
  2.suggesting similar column names,
  3.clarifying that the requested column does not exist,
  4.improving beginner usability.

This enhancement would improve:

  debugging experience,
  developer productivity,
  accessibility for new Pandas users.

### Alternative Solutions

# Proposed Solution

Locate the internal column access logic (likely in:

pandas/core/frame.py

)

and replace the generic:

raise KeyError(key)

with a more informative exception message.

Example:

raise KeyError(
    f"Column '{key}' not found in DataFrame. "
    f"Available columns: {list(self.columns)}"
)

### Addit

title: BUG:  `groupby` cumprod/cummin/cummax returns `Float64` for nullable integers when group key contains `NA`
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
      "key": ["a", None, "a"],
      "val": pd.array([2, 3, 4], dtype="Int64"),
})

g = df.groupby("key")

print(g["val"].cumsum().dtype)   # Int64   (correct)
print(g["val"].cumprod().dtype)  # Float64 (expected Int64)
print(g["val"].cummin().dtype)   # Float64 (expected Int64)
print(g["val"].cummax().dtype)   # Float64 (expected Int64)
```

### Issue Description

`groupby().cumprod()`, `groupby().cummin()`, and `groupby().cummax()` return `Float64` instead of preserving the nullable integer dtype, e.g. `Int64`, when the group key contains NA values and `dropna=True`. 

`groupby().cumsum()` is not affected ( it already handles this case correctly following #58811 ) . The same handling is missing from group_cumprod and group_cummin_max.

### Expected Behavior

The dtype of the output should match the input nullable integer dtype. 

### Installed Versions

<details>

INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------
commit                : 2a7587fed01e1b7d523d59281b92e1a32a20fd9e
python                : 3.12.12
python-bits           : 64
OS                    : Windows

Version               : 10.0.26200
machine               : AMD64
processor             : Intel64 Family 6 Model 183 Stepping 1, GenuineIntel

title: BUG: Conversion from np.ma.masked_array to pd.array does not preserve missing values
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

a = np.ma.array([1, 2, 3, 4], mask=[False, True, False, True])

# BUG: mask is ignored completely
pd.array(a)
# Output:
# <IntegerArray>
# [1, 2, 3, 4]
# Length: 4, dtype: Int64

# For comparison, pd.Series handles the mask but changes dtype to float
pd.Series(a)
# Output:
# 0 1.0
# 1 NaN
# 2 3.0
# 3 NaN
# dtype: float64
```

### Issue Description

NumPy [masked arrays](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/maskedarray.generic.html#what-is-a-masked-array) are used to define arrays with missing values, similar to pandas extension arrays. However, converting to `pd.array` currently ignores the mask completely and masked values appear as regular values.

Converting to `pd.Series` does respect the missing values, but the dtype is converted to `float` and the original dtype is not preserved in all cases.

This behavior is inconsistent with user expectations and with how `pd.Series` handles masked arrays.

The same issue occurs with string masked arrays where the mask is also ignored by `pd.array`.

### Expected Behavior

Masked values should be converted to pd.NA and the appropriate nullable dtype should be preserved, such as Int64 or string, instead of ignoring the mask or coercing to float.

>>> pd.array(a)
<IntegerArray>
[1, <NA>, 3, <NA>]
Length: 4, dtype: Int64

### Installed Versions

pandas: 3.0.0
numpy: 2.3.5
python: 3.12.9

title: DOC: Missing docstring for enable_data_resource_formatter in printing module
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on `main` [here](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/)


### Location of the documentation

pandas/io/formats/printing.py

### Documentation problem

The function `enable_data_resource_formatter` in `pandas/io/formats/printing.py` does not have a docstring describing its purpose or behavior. 

This function performs non-trivial logic, including:
- Detecting whether the code is running in an IPython environment
- Dynamically registering a formatter for the `application/vnd.dataresource+json` mimetype
- Enabling or disabling that  formatter based on the 'enable' parameter

Without documentation, it is unclear:
- What this formatter is used for
- When this function should be called

This makes the function difficult to understand and maintain for contributors. 

### Suggested fix for documentation

The current implementation currently has no docstring, so developers must read and interpret the source code to understand its purpose and behavior.

Adding a docstring would improve the documentation by:
- Clearly explaining the purpose of the function 
- Describing the meaning of the `enable` parameter

This makes the function easier to understand, reduces the likelihood of misuse, and improves maintainability for contributors.

title: DEP: update of fsspec 2026.4 broke compressed csv reading
body:
From https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65433, the update of fsspec from 2026.2.0 to 2026.4 caused some tests to fail (that PR added a temporary upper pin).

From looking at the PRs included in that release, this is likely caused by https://github.com/fsspec/filesystem_spec/pull/2012

Example failure (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/actions/runs/25182285458/job/73830335550):

```

title: PERF: skip to_datetime cache=True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help
body:
#### Summary

`to_datetime(..., cache=True)` (the default) unconditionally runs the
`_maybe_cache` machinery — `Series(dtype=object)` construction +
`should_cache` which iterates up to 500 elements of the input — even on
inputs where `_convert_listlike_datetimes` is going to dispatch to a
non-parsing path or a path where caching has been measured to never help.
On these inputs `cache=True` is strictly slower than `cache=False`, by
margins ranging from a few percent to **~520×** at the worst.

All five "no-help" conditions are identifiable in O(1) time, before any
sampling, just by looking at `arg.dtype`, the `unit` kwarg, and the
`format` kwarg.

#### Affected input shapes (all O(1) to detect)

1. `unit is not None` — input is dispatched to `_to_datetime_with_unit`,
   which is a vectorized cast; caching cannot help.
2. `arg.dtype` is `np.dtype("M8[*]")` — already datetime64; `_convert_listlike_datetimes` short-circuits.
3. `arg.dtype` is `DatetimeTZDtype` — already tz-aware; short-circuits.
4. `arg.dtype` is `ArrowDtype` with `type is Timestamp` — short-circuits.
5. `format is not None and format != "mixed"` — `array_strptime` is fast
   enough that `pd.unique()` + `Series.map()` costs as much as just
   re-parsing; `cache=True` is never a measurable win on this path.

#### Reproducer

```python
import time
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd


def best(fn, k=5):
    fn()  # warmup
    times = []
    for _ in range(k):
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        fn()
        times.append(time.perf_counter() - t0)
    return min(times)


def bench(label, arg, **kwargs):
    t_off = best(lambda: pd.to_datetime(arg, cache=False, **kwargs))
    t_on = best(lambda: pd.to_datetime(arg, cache=True, **kwargs))
    print(f"{label:<35s}  off={t_off*1000:7.2f}ms  on={t_on*1000:7.2f}ms  on/off={t_on/t_off:.2f}x")


n = 100_000
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

arr_unit = np.arange(n, dtype="int64")
arr_dt64 = pd.date_range("2020-01-01", periods=n, freq="s").to_numpy()
idx_tz = p

title: BUG:  Index `&` operator performs bitwise AND instead of set intersection for int64 dtype in v2.2.3
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
### Pandas Version
2.2.3

### Python Version
3.12 (请替换成你的实际版本)

### Operating System
Windows

### Description
When using the `&` operator on two `pd.Index` objects containing integers, the operation returns the NumPy bitwise AND result instead of the set intersection.
The `.intersection()` method works as expected, confirming that the `&` operator is broken.

### Reproducible Code

import pandas as pd
indA = pd.Index([1, 3, 5, 7, 9])
indB = pd.Index([2, 3, 5, 7, 11])
print(indA & indB)
```

### Issue Description

2.2.3

### Python Version

### Operating System
Windows

### Description
When using the `&` operator on two `pd.Index` objects containing integers, the operation returns the NumPy bitwise AND result instead of the set intersection.
The `.intersection()` method works as expected, confirming that the `&` operator is broken.

### Reproducible Code
```python

indB = pd.Index([2, 3, 5, 7, 11])
print(indA & indB)

Actual Behavior

title: DOC: `to_numeric` "Returns" is missing Index and scalars, may have other problems
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that the issue still exists on the latest versions of the docs on `main` [here](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/)


### Location of the documentation

https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/reference/api/pandas.to_numeric.html

> **Returns:**
> **ret**
> Numeric if parsing succeeded. Return type depends on input. Series if Series, otherwise ndarray.

### Documentation problem

The documentation is missing these return types:

### Index

1-d specifically

Example:
```py
>>> pd.to_numeric(pd.Index(['0']))
Index([0], dtype='int64')
```

Source:
https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/be0642f7cb8900282a43b53cd50cf00a05161ed6/pandas/core/tools/numeric.py#L318-L323

### Scalars

Example:
```py
>>> pd.to_numeric('0')
np.int64(0)
```

Source:
https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/be0642f7cb8900282a43b53cd50cf00a05161ed6/pandas/core/tools/numeric.py#L202-L210

https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/be0642f7cb8900282a43b53cd50cf00a05161ed6/pandas/core/tools/numeric.py#L324-L325

### Additionally

"Numeric if parsing succeeded" implies there's a different return type if parsing failed, but the function would actually **raise** in that case (instead of returning), if I understand correctly. Or it would return NaN, but that's still a numeric type, even though it's called "not a number".

Also, the `dtype_backend` parameter says: "Back-end data type applied to the resultant **DataFrame**", but `to_numeric` can't return a DataFrame as far as I know. I tried it just to confirm:
```py
>>> pd.to_numeric(pd.DataFrame([0]))
TypeError: arg must be a list, tuple, 1-d array, or Series

>>> pd.to_numeric(pd.DataFrame([0]), dtype_backend='pyarrow')
TypeError: arg must be a list, tuple, 1-d array, or Series

>>> pd.to_numeric(pd.DataFrame([0]), dtype_backend='numpy_nullable')
TypeError: arg must be a list, tuple, 1-d array, or Series
```

### Suggested fix for documentation

I don't understand all the ins and outs of the function, but I

title: BUG: df.query() returning an empty dataframe on this condition - col1 in (col2, col3)
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({'a': [1, 2, 3], 'b': [1, 4, 9], 'c': [1, 8, 27]})

# Scenario 1
print(df.query('b <= a <= c'))
# Returns the first row (Expected)

# Scenario 2
print(df.query('b + 1 <= a <= c'))
# Returns an empty dataframe (Expected)

# Scenario 3
print(df.query('b in (a, c)'))
# Returns an empty dataframe (Shouldn't this also return the first row)

df = pd.DataFrame({'a': ['a', 'a', 'b'], 'b': ['a', 'b', 'c'], 'c': ['b', 'b', 'c']})

# Scenario 4
print(df.query('b in (a, c)'))
# ArrowInvalid error
```

### Issue Description

My gripe is obviously with scenarios 3 and 4. I think the outcome should be consistent and independent of the datatypes of the columns. 

### Expected Behavior

Both the scenarios should either:
- return a filtered dataframe by using a dynamic expression which evaluates column tokens on both the left and the right hand sides (the closest example would be scenarios 1 and 2, although they have 3 operands)
- or throw the same error

### Installed Versions

Can't paste as I've tested the sample code in a different machine, but I've upgraded pandas to 3.0.2 before doing so.

title: BUG: Series.combine_first fails when names are of type Timestamp
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

s1 = pd.Series([0], name=pd.to_datetime('2026'))
s2 = pd.Series([1,2], name=pd.to_datetime('2027'))
s3 = pd.Series([1,3], name=pd.to_datetime('2025'))

print(s1.combine_first(s2))
print(s1.combine_first(s3))
```

### Issue Description

`s1.combine_first(s3)` fails because `s3.name` is a datetime anterior to `s1.name`.

ERROR :

> Traceback (most recent call last):
>     print(s1.combine_first(s3))
>           ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^
>   File ".venv\Lib\site-packages\pandas\core\series.py", line 3441, in combine_first
>     combined = concat([this, other])
>   File ".venv\Lib\site-packages\pandas\core\reshape\concat.py", line 489, in concat
>     orig = result.index if orig_axis == 1 else result.columns
>                                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
>   File ".venv\Lib\site-packages\pandas\core\generic.py", line 6206, in __getattr__
>     return object.__getattribute__(self, name)
>            ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^
> AttributeError: 'Series' object has no attribute 'columns'


It may come from this behavior in `pd.concat`, line 420:

> if (
>             intersect
>             or any(not isinstance(index, DatetimeIndex) for index in non_concat_axis)
>             or all(prev is curr for prev, curr in pairwise(non_concat_axis))
>             or (
>                 all(
>                     prev[-1] <= curr[0] and prev.is_monotonic_increasing
>                     for prev, curr in pairwise(non_concat_axis)
>                     if not prev.empty and not curr.empty
>                 )

title: BUG: DataFrame/Series.rename silently downcasts nullable index/column dtypes to NumPy dtypes
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame(
      {"val": [1, 2, 3]},
      index=pd.Index(pd.array([1, 2, 3], dtype="Int64"), name="id"),
  )
print(df.index.dtype)
print(df.rename({1: 9}).index.dtype)
```

### Issue Description

`DataFrame.rename()` and `Series.rename()` do not preserve nullable extension dtypes on index/column labels. In the reproduced cases with nullable integer and floating dtypes, renaming labels silently downcasts the result to the corresponding NumPy dtype.

The root cause appears to be `Index._transform_index()`, which reconstructs the transformed index from a plain Python list instead of preserving/casting through the original `ExtensionArray` dtype.

### Expected Behavior

`rename()` should preserve the original index dtype after relabeling.

### Installed Versions

<details>

INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------

title: BUG: [REGRESSION] to_csv cannot handle FloatingArrays with np.nan, even with distinguish_nan_and_na=True
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

s = pd.Series((1, pd.NA, 0), index=["a","b","c"], dtype=pd.Float64Dtype(), name="x")
with pd.option_context("future.distinguish_nan_and_na", True):
    s = s/s # creates a series with both NA and NaN
    print(s.to_csv(sep=",", mode="w"))
```

### Issue Description

The above code raises the following error:
```
ArrowTypeError: Expected bytes, got a 'numpy.float64' object
```

A similar code, worked as expected with Pandas<3 (i.e. `pandas=2.3.2`, with `PyArrow=21.0.0` just like for pandas3):
```python
import pandas as pd

s = pd.Series((1, pd.NA, 0), index=["a","b","c"], dtype=pd.Float64Dtype(), name="x")
s = s/s # creates a series with both NA and NaN
print(s.to_csv(sep=",", mode="w"))
```
prints: 
```
,x
a,1.0
b,
c,nan
```

### Expected Behavior

I would expect to have the same behaviour as Pandas2. Even more so if `distinguish_nan_and_na=True`.

Note that Pandas2 distinguished between NA and NaN (i.e. empty string vs `"nan"` string)

### Installed Versions

<details>

INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------
commit                : ab90747e3dae0e69b1bdbf083820b8075689b34b
python                : 3.13.13
python-bits           : 64
OS                    : Linux
OS-release            : 6.17.0-20-generic
Version               : #20~24.04.1-Ubuntu SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu Mar 19 01:28:37 UTC 2
machine               : x86_64
processor             : x86_64
byteorder             : little
LC_ALL                : None
LANG                  : en_GB.UTF-8
LOCALE                : en_GB.UTF-8

title: CI regression: Doc Build and pre-commit jobs failing across multiple PRs
body:
### Describe the bug

The following CI jobs are failing across multiple unrelated PRs:

- Doc Build and Upload
- Code Checks / Docstring validation, typing, and other manual pre-commit hooks

Failures appear unrelated to PR contents and occur during environment setup/build.

---

### Evidence

Observed failures on:

- https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65212
- https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65224
- https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65225

All show failures in:

- Doc Build and Upload
- Docstring validation, typing, and other manual pre-commit hooks

---

### Error message

From the Doc Build job:

../../meson.build:2:0: ERROR: Compiler cython cannot compile programs.

This occurs during the Meson sanity check before pandas builds.

---

### Expected behavior

CI jobs should pass for unrelated PRs unless affected by code changes.

---

### Additional context

Since the same checks are failing across multiple unrelated PRs,
this appears to be a CI environment regression rather than a
PR-specific issue.

title: BUG: 1-element pd.NA boolean arrays are all() but not any()
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
s1 = pd.Series({"a": pd.NA}, dtype="boolean")
s2 = pd.Series({"a": pd.NA, "b": False}, dtype="boolean")
s1.all(), s1.any(), s2.all(), s2.any()
```

### Issue Description

Following #49828, I understand that `or`, `and` and `not` operators might not be correctly implemented for `pd.NA`. This, however, creates some funny behaviour when working with arrays of length 1 vs those of length >1.

The above examples outputs:
`(np.True_, np.False_, np.False_, np.False_)`

### Expected Behavior

```
>>> s1.all()
np.False_
```

title: BUG: `DataFrame.agg([...], numeric_only=True)` returns `NaN` for non-numeric columns instead of excluding them
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
      "A": [1, 2, 3],
      "B": [1.0, 2.0, 3.0],
      "C": ["foo", "bar", "baz"], })

result = df.agg(["sum", "mean"], numeric_only=True)
print(result)
```

### Issue Description

`DataFrame.agg([...], numeric_only=True)` returns `NaN` for non-numeric columns instead of excluding them. This affects `DataFrame.agg` with a list of string reduction names and `numeric_only=True`, including reductions such as `sum`, `mean`, `std`, `var`, `sem`, `median`, `prod`, `min`, and `max`, on DataFrames with non-numeric columns such as `object`, `datetime64[ns]`, `timedelta64[ns]`, `StringDtype`, `CategoricalDtype`, and `DatetimeTZDtype`.


### Expected Behavior

Non-numeric columns should be excluded from the result when `DataFrame.agg([...], numeric_only=True)` is used, rather than being included as all `NaN`.


### Installed Versions

<details>

title: API: Index.take discards fill_value and raises unnecessarily
body:
`Index.take` has two bugs when `allow_fill=True` and `fill_value` is passed explicitly:

### 1. `fill_value` is silently discarded

The caller's `fill_value` is ignored; `self._na_value` is always substituted:

```python
# Note: we discard fill_value and use self._na_value, only relevant
#  in the case where allow_fill is True and fill_value is not None
taken = algos.take(
    values, indices, allow_fill=allow_fill, fill_value=self._na_value
)
```

### 2. `_can_hold_na` gate raises on integer dtypes

```python
>>> pd.Index([1, 2, 3]).take([0, -1], allow_fill=True, fill_value=99)
ValueError: Unable to fill values because Index cannot contain NA
```

The caller provided a valid fill value that the dtype can hold, but `Index.take` rejects it because `_can_hold_na` is False for integer dtypes.

### Consequences

These force internal callers to bypass `Index.take` and work on the underlying arrays directly:

- **`frame.py` idxmin/idxmax**: `algorithms.take(index._values, ...)` instead of `index.take(...)`
- **`reshape/merge.py`**: appends a sentinel value to the index, with a comment: *"We do not use allow_fill and fill_value because it throws a ValueError on integer indices"*
- **`indexes/base.py` reset_index**: take-then-putmask as two operations
- **`io/formats/excel.py`**: guards `allow_fill` on `levels._can_hold_na`
- **`indexes/multi.py`**: `algos.take_nd(lev._values, ...)` instead of `lev.take(...)`

### Proposed fix

- Respect the caller's `fill_value`
- Remove the `_can_hold_na` gate (let the underlying array handle type promotion)
- Simplify the workaround sites

This is backward-compatible: the existing gate (`allow_fill=True` + `fill_value is None` → numpy-style take) is preserved, so bare `idx.take(indices)` is unchanged.

Separately, there are questions about the right long-term defaults for `allow_fill` (currently `True` for backward compat but arguably should be `False` to match `ExtensionArray.take`), but that would require a deprecation cycle and is out

title: ENH: Simplify reading vertically stacked CSV tables
body:
### Feature Type

- [x] Adding new functionality to pandas

- [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas

- [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas


### Problem Description

The overall goal is reading vertically stacked CSV files, with multiple tables.
There is no difficulty in writing such a file with pandas (just append) or reading and splitting horizontally stacked CSV files (but they can be inefficient and more complicated to write).
This is not technically a CSV format (according to RFC 4180, since column sizes differ) but a very slight generalisation of it.
It doesn't really have a name, but has been re-invented several times, with different ways of determining where to split the table.

I think the only form that really needs supporting are known specified lines, e.g. [[''], ['Col1', 'Col2']] would split on blank rows but also on Col1,Col2 rows. I had considered "when the number of columns changes", but I think that's a strictly less useful format (you can't have multiple tables with the same number of columns consecutively), and it could be extended in later if wanted.

### Feature Description

Possible solutions I can think of (any one of the following):
#### A) Part of TextFileReader chunking
   
   This would require the ability to reset the columns state of the reader (either by the user or as part of the implementation).
   If that is easy to do, then this might be the most straightforward way of implementing this feature.

   The simplest way of doing this is adding an option chunk_on = None, which can be set to a list of row values (permitted to include the blank row).
   Importantly the presence of this does not just cause the chunks to be broken off but also the number, header, names and types of columns to be reset (if not specified as a parameter) as if reading a new file. (But it does not reset if chunking because of chunksize, so the two are compatible.)

   If skip_blank_lines is True, then blank lines can end (if [''] is in chunk_on

title: BUG: pd.to_timedelta ignores unit value for certain float values
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
pd.to_timedelta([1.0, 1.01], unit="s")
```

### Issue Description

`pd.to_timedelta()` does ignore the unit argument in certain cases.

For the example above we get in `pandas<3.0`:

```
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:00:01', '0 days 00:00:01.010000'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
```

but starting with `pandas>=3.0` we get:

```
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:00:00.000000001', '0 days 00:00:01.010000'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
```

It does not happen when providing a `numpy` array as input:

```python
>>> pd.to_timedelta(np.array([1.0, 1.01]), unit="s")
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:00:01', '0 days 00:00:01.010000'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
```

Most likely it was introduced by #63533 

### Expected Behavior

It should return

```
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:00:01', '0 days 00:00:01.010000'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
```

### Installed Versions

<details>

INSTALLED VERSIONS          
------------------

title: BUG: `groupby(..., as_index=False)[col].ohlc()` returns group keys in the index
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

``` python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "key": ["A", "A", "B", "B"],
    "price": [1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0],
})

result = df.groupby("key", as_index=False)["price"].ohlc()

print(result)
print()
print("Index:", result.index.tolist())
print("Columns:", result.columns.tolist())
```

### Issue Description

`df.groupby("key", as_index=False)["col"].ohlc()` ignores `as_index=False`: the group key is returned in the index instead of as a column. Other aggregations such as `sum`, `mean`, `min`, and `max` correctly honor `as_index=False`.

### Expected Behavior

with `as_index=False`, the group key should be returned as a column.

### Installed Versions

title: ENH: Add DataFrame.memory_report() for actionable dtype optimization suggestions
body: ### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description DataFrame.memory_usage() tells you how much memory a DataFrame uses, but gives no guidance on why or what to do about it. Users who want to optimize memory must: - Manually inspect each column's dtype - Know the integer/float downcast rules by heart - Manually check cardinality for object columns - Write their own loops to apply changes This is a very common pain point — especially for data engineers and analysts working with medium-to-large datasets who hit memory pressure and have no built-in tool to diagnose it. ### Feature Description A new method DataFrame.memory_report() that: 1. Analyzes every column's current dtype and actual value range 2. Suggests the smallest safe dtype for each column 3. Explains why each suggestion is safe (value range, cardinality ratio) 4. Returns a rich MemoryReport object that is both human-readable and 5. machine-readable 6. Never modifies the DataFrame — purely diagnostic, zero side effects def memory_report( self, verbose: bool = True, category_threshold: float = 0.5, check_datetimes: bool = True, ) -> MemoryReport: """ Analyze memory usage and suggest safe dtype optimizations. Parameters ---------- verbose : bool, default True If True, print the full report to stdout. category_threshold : float, default 0.5 Suggest ``category`` dtype when unique ratio is below this value. For example, 0.5 means fewer than 50% unique values → suggest category. check_datetimes : bool, default True Attempt to detect datetime-parseable strings in object columns. Samples up to 200 rows to avoid scanning very large columns. Returns -------
